title: Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis Fundamentals
description: Understand the relationships between cost, volume, and profit

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CVP analysis is the workhorse of managerial accounting. CMAs use it to answer critical questions: How many units must we sell to break even? What price point maximizes profit? How do cost changes affect the bottom line? This is HIGH-WEIGHT EXAM CONTENT!

sections.title: CVP Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Basic CVP equation:**
Profit = (Price × Quantity) - (VC per unit × Quantity) - Fixed Costs

**Or:**
Profit = (P - V)Q - F

**Where:**
• P = Selling price per unit
• V = Variable cost per unit
• Q = Quantity sold
• F = Total fixed costs

**Key relationship:**
Each unit sold contributes (P - V) toward fixed costs and profit.

sections.title: Contribution Margin
sections.content:
**Per-unit CM:** Selling Price - Variable Cost = CM per unit

**CM Ratio:** CM per unit / Selling Price = CM%

**Example:**
Price: $100, Variable cost: $60
CM per unit: $100 - $60 = **$40**
CM ratio: $40 / $100 = **40%**

**Interpretation:**
Each unit contributes $40 toward fixed costs.
Each $1 of revenue contributes $0.40 toward fixed costs.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CM Relationships
sections.content:
**"CVP-CM is KEY"**

**C**ontribution = Price - Variable cost
**V**olume × CM = Total contribution
**P**rofit = Total CM - Fixed costs

**CM is the CRITICAL number!**
Once fixed costs are covered, every unit's CM is pure profit.

Higher CM ratio = More operating leverage = More sensitive to volume changes

sections.title: Break-Even Analysis
sections.content:
**Break-even in UNITS:**
BE Units = Fixed Costs / CM per unit

**Break-even in DOLLARS:**
BE Dollars = Fixed Costs / CM Ratio

**Example:**
Fixed costs: $200,000
CM per unit: $40
CM ratio: 40%

BE Units: $200,000 / $40 = **5,000 units**
BE Dollars: $200,000 / 0.40 = **$500,000**

**Verify:** 5,000 × $100 = $500,000 ✓

sections.title: Target Profit Analysis
sections.content:
**Units for target profit:**
Units = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / CM per unit

**Dollars for target profit:**
Dollars = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / CM Ratio

**Example:**
Target profit: $80,000
Units = ($200,000 + $80,000) / $40 = **7,000 units**

**For after-tax profit target:**
Pre-tax profit needed = After-tax target / (1 - Tax rate)

If $60,000 after-tax desired, tax rate 25%:
Pre-tax = $60,000 / 0.75 = $80,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Variable vs. Fixed
sections.content:
**Everything in CVP depends on proper cost classification!**

**Variable:** Changes proportionally with volume
(Materials, commissions, piece-rate labor)

**Fixed:** Stays constant within relevant range
(Rent, salaries, depreciation)

**Mixed costs:** Separate into fixed and variable components!

**Wrong classification → Wrong analysis → Wrong decisions**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CVP: Profit = (P-V)Q - F
Contribution Margin = Price - Variable Cost
CM Ratio = CM / Price (% of revenue toward fixed costs)
Break-even Units = Fixed Costs / CM per unit
Break-even Dollars = Fixed Costs / CM Ratio
Target profit: Add desired profit to fixed costs
For after-tax targets, gross up: Pre-tax = After-tax / (1-t)

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells a product for $50 per unit with variable costs of $30 per unit. Fixed costs are $100,000. If the company wants to achieve a profit of $50,000, how many units must they sell?
options: 2,500 units
5,000 units
7,500 units
10,000 units
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To calculate the target sales in units, we use the formula: Units = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / Contribution Margin per unit.  In this case, Units = ($100,000 + $50,000) / ($50 - $30) = $150,000 / $20 = 7,500 units.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include the desired profit in your calculation of total costs.

sections.title: Test Your Knowledge of the Break-Even Formula

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the break-even point in dollars?
reveal.answer: Break-even in Dollars = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin Ratio

sections.title: Contribution Margin Ratio vs. Contribution Margin per Unit

rows.label: Definition
values: The percentage of each sales dollar that is available to cover fixed costs and contribute to profit.
The amount of revenue remaining after deducting variable costs from the selling price of one unit.

rows.label: Formula
values: Contribution Margin per Unit / Selling Price per Unit
Selling Price per Unit - Variable Cost per Unit

rows.label: Units
values: Percentage
Currency ($)

title: Multi-Product CVP Analysis
description: Apply CVP analysis to companies with multiple products

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Most companies sell multiple products with different margins. The mix matters! CMAs must understand how product mix affects overall profitability and break-even. Shifting toward higher-margin products can dramatically improve results.

sections.title: Sales Mix Concept
sections.content:
**Sales mix:** Proportion of each product in total sales

**Example:**
Product A: 60% of units sold
Product B: 40% of units sold

**Why mix matters:**
Changing the mix changes overall profitability!

**Shift toward higher-CM products:**
→ Overall CM increases
→ Break-even decreases
→ Profits increase

sections.title: Weighted Average Contribution Margin
sections.content:
**WACM = Σ(CM per unit × Mix %)**

**From the example:**
WACM = ($20 × 0.60) + ($40 × 0.40)
WACM = $12 + $16 = **$28**

**Break-even in "bundles":**
BE Bundles = Fixed Costs / WACM

If FC = $280,000:
BE = $280,000 / $28 = **10,000 bundles**

**Then allocate by mix:**
Standard: 10,000 × 60% = 6,000 units
Premium: 10,000 × 40% = 4,000 units

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Multi-Product CVP
sections.content:
**"MIX it up with WACM"**

**Step 1:** Calculate CM for each product
**Step 2:** Weight by sales MIX
**Step 3:** Sum to get WACM
**Step 4:** Divide FC by WACM for break-even bundles
**Step 5:** Allocate bundles back by mix

**WACM assumes CONSTANT mix - this is critical!**

sections.title: Changing the Mix
sections.content:
**What if mix changes to 50/50?**

New WACM = ($20 × 0.50) + ($40 × 0.50)
= $10 + $20 = **$30**

New BE = $280,000 / $30 = **9,333 bundles**

**Lower break-even because we shifted toward higher-margin Premium!**

**Key insight:**
Management can improve profitability by:
• Marketing high-margin products
• Commission incentives on premium products
• Pricing strategies to shift mix

sections.title: Contribution Margin per Dollar of Revenue
sections.content:
**Alternative: Use weighted CM ratio**

**Weighted CM Ratio = Σ(CM% × Revenue Mix %)**

**Example:**
Standard: CM ratio = 40%, Revenue mix = 42.9%
Premium: CM ratio = 50%, Revenue mix = 57.1%

Weighted CM% = (0.40 × 0.429) + (0.50 × 0.571)
= 0.172 + 0.286 = **45.8%**

**BE Dollars = FC / Weighted CM%**
= $280,000 / 0.458 = **$611,354**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Mix Given in Units vs. Dollars
sections.content:
**Pay attention to how mix is stated!**

**Unit mix:** Use CM per unit, calculate WACM
**Dollar/revenue mix:** Use CM ratio, calculate weighted CM%

**These can give different percentages!**

Example: If Premium costs 2× Standard:
• Unit mix: 60/40
• Dollar mix: Different (more revenue from Premium)

**Read the problem carefully!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sales mix affects overall profitability
WACM = Weighted Average CM (by unit mix)
BE Bundles = Fixed Costs / WACM
Allocate bundles back to products by mix percentage
Shift toward higher-CM products to improve results
CVP assumes CONSTANT mix (key assumption)
Unit mix ≠ Dollar mix when prices differ

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X sells two products: Alpha and Beta. Alpha has a contribution margin of $30 per unit and represents 30% of the sales mix, while Beta has a contribution margin of $50 per unit and represents 70% of the sales mix. If the company shifts its sales mix to 50% Alpha and 50% Beta, and total sales in units remain constant, what will be the impact on the company's overall profitability, assuming fixed costs remain unchanged?
options: Overall profitability will increase.
Overall profitability will decrease.
Overall profitability will remain the same.
The impact on profitability cannot be determined without knowing the fixed costs.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The weighted average contribution margin (WACM) initially is (0.30 * $30) + (0.70 * $50) = $44. With the shift, the WACM becomes (0.50 * $30) + (0.50 * $50) = $40. Since the total sales in units remain constant and the WACM decreases, the overall profitability will decrease. A lower WACM with the same unit volume means less contribution margin to cover fixed costs and generate profit. Therefore, the correct answer is B.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the weighted average contribution margin (WACM) before and after the shift in sales mix.  Consider how changes in WACM impact overall profitability.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the break-even point in bundles in a multi-product CVP analysis?
reveal.answer: Break-Even Point in Bundles = Fixed Costs / Weighted Average Contribution Margin (WACM)

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells three products: X, Y, and Z. Their sales mix is 20%, 30%, and 50% respectively. The contribution margins per unit are $15, $20, and $25 respectively. If the company's total fixed costs are $150,000, what is the break-even point in 'bundles' of X, Y, and Z?
options: 5,000 bundles
6,000 bundles
7,500 bundles
10,000 bundles
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the WACM: (0.20 * $15) + (0.30 * $20) + (0.50 * $25) = $3 + $6 + $12.5 = $21.5. Then, calculate the break-even point in bundles: $150,000 / $21.5 = 6,976.74 bundles. The closest answer is 7,000 bundles.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the Weighted Average Contribution Margin (WACM) before calculating the break-even point.

title: Operating Leverage
description: Understand how cost structure affects profit sensitivity

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Operating leverage is a double-edged sword. High fixed costs magnify profits when sales are good but magnify losses when sales decline. CMAs analyze cost structure to understand risk, plan capacity, and evaluate strategic alternatives.

sections.title: Operating Leverage Concept
sections.content:
**Operating leverage:** The extent to which fixed costs are used in operations

**High operating leverage:**
• High fixed costs, low variable costs
• Small sales increase → Large profit increase
• Small sales decrease → Large profit decrease
• More risk!

**Low operating leverage:**
• Low fixed costs, high variable costs
• Profit changes proportionally with sales
• Less risky

sections.title: Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)
sections.content:
**DOL = Contribution Margin / Operating Income**

**Or:**
DOL = % Change in EBIT / % Change in Sales

**Example:**
Sales: $500,000
VC: $300,000 (60%)
CM: $200,000 (40%)
FC: $150,000
Operating Income: $50,000

DOL = $200,000 / $50,000 = **4.0**

**Interpretation:** 1% increase in sales → 4% increase in EBIT

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DOL
sections.content:
**"DOL = CM / OI"**

**D**egree of **O**perating **L**everage = **C**ontribution **M**argin / **O**perating **I**ncome

**Higher DOL = Higher risk!**

DOL is highest near break-even (OI near zero)
DOL decreases as you move away from break-even

**At break-even, DOL is undefined (divide by zero)!**

sections.title: Strategic Implications
sections.content:
**When to choose high operating leverage:**
• Confident in sales growth
• Stable demand
• Want to maximize upside

**When to choose low operating leverage:**
• Uncertain demand
• New market entry
• Risk-averse management

**Trade-off:**
Automation → Higher fixed costs → Higher OL
Outsourcing → Higher variable costs → Lower OL

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: DOL Changes with Volume
sections.content:
**DOL is NOT constant!**

• DOL is highest near break-even
• DOL decreases as volume increases
• DOL approaches 1 at very high volumes

**Always calculate DOL at the specific volume given!**

DOL = CM / OI

As OI grows (farther from break-even), DOL shrinks.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Operating leverage measures fixed cost intensity
DOL = CM / Operating Income
High DOL: Small sales change → Large profit change
DOL is highest near break-even
High fixed costs = High OL = High risk
Automation increases OL; Outsourcing decreases OL
Choose OL based on demand stability and risk tolerance

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X is considering two different operational strategies: Strategy A, which relies heavily on automation and has high fixed costs and low variable costs; and Strategy B, which uses more manual labor and has lower fixed costs but higher variable costs. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the risk and potential reward associated with these strategies?
options: Strategy A has lower risk and lower potential reward compared to Strategy B.
Strategy A has higher risk and higher potential reward compared to Strategy B.
Strategy B has higher risk and higher potential reward compared to Strategy A.
Strategy A and Strategy B have the same risk and potential reward.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Strategy A, with its high fixed costs and low variable costs, has high operating leverage. This means a small increase in sales can lead to a large increase in profits (higher potential reward). However, it also means a small decrease in sales can lead to a large decrease in profits (higher risk). Strategy B has lower operating leverage and thus lower risk and reward.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how fixed costs affect profitability when sales fluctuate.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)?
reveal.answer:
DOL = Contribution Margin / Operating Income  
OR 
DOL = % Change in EBIT / % Change in Sales

sections.title: Calculating the Impact of Sales Change

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) of 2.5. If the company expects sales to increase by 8%, what is the expected percentage change in EBIT?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The formula DOL = % Change in EBIT / % Change in Sales can be rearranged to solve for % Change in EBIT: % Change in EBIT = DOL * % Change in Sales. Therefore, % Change in EBIT = 2.5 * 8% = 20%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the relationship between DOL, % change in sales, and % change in EBIT.

title: Relevant Cost Analysis
description: Identify costs relevant to specific decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Not all costs matter for every decision. CMAs must identify which costs are relevant to a specific choice and ignore sunk costs that shouldn't affect the decision. This skill is fundamental to all short-term decisions covered in this section!

sections.title: What Makes a Cost Relevant?
sections.content:
**A cost is relevant if it is:**

1. **Future-oriented:** Will be incurred in the future
2. **Differential:** Differs between alternatives

**Both conditions must be met!**

**Example:**
Buy vs. Make decision
• Raw materials cost: Relevant (differs between alternatives)
• Factory rent: Not relevant if paid either way
• Past R&D cost: Not relevant (already spent)

sections.title: Types of Costs
rows: Can be eliminated with a choice

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Relevant Costs
sections.content:
**"FD = Future & Different"**

**F**uture: Will be incurred after the decision
**D**ifferent: Changes based on the choice

**Sunk costs are NEVER relevant!**
(No matter how much you spent or regret it)

**The question is always: "What changes if we choose differently?"**

sections.title: Opportunity Cost
sections.content:
**Definition:** The benefit foregone by choosing one alternative over another

**Example:**
Machine can produce Product A or Product B (not both)
Product A contribution: $50,000
Product B contribution: $60,000

If you choose A, opportunity cost = **$60,000** (lost B contribution)

**Opportunity costs are:**
• Implicit (not in accounting records)
• But absolutely relevant to decisions!
• The "next best alternative" value

sections.title: Sunk Cost Trap
sections.content:
**Common mistakes:**

**"We've invested $1 million, we can't stop now!"**
→ The $1M is gone either way. Focus on FUTURE costs/benefits.

**"The equipment cost $500,000..."**
→ For decisions, only future costs matter.

**"We need to recover our investment."**
→ Past investment is irrelevant to optimal future decisions.

**The relevant question:**
"Given where we are NOW, what's the best path FORWARD?"

sections.title: Differential Analysis Framework
sections.content:
**Compare alternatives by focusing on differences:**

**Step 1:** Identify all costs/revenues for each alternative
**Step 2:** Cross out items that are the same
**Step 3:** Analyze only the differential amounts
**Step 4:** Include opportunity costs
**Step 5:** Choose alternative with highest net benefit

**Shortcut:** Only analyze costs/revenues that CHANGE!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Allocated Costs
sections.content:
**Allocated fixed costs are usually NOT relevant!**

**Example:**
Product A is allocated $50,000 of corporate overhead
Should we drop Product A?

**Wrong:** Include the $50,000 (it's assigned to A)
**Right:** Will the $50,000 disappear if we drop A?

If overhead continues regardless → NOT relevant
If overhead can be eliminated → Relevant

**Always ask: "Does this cost CHANGE?"**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Relevant costs are Future AND Differential
Sunk costs are NEVER relevant (already spent)
Opportunity costs ARE relevant (foregone benefit)
Focus on what CHANGES between alternatives
Allocated costs are relevant only if avoidable
The past is irrelevant - focus on future cash flows
Ask: "What changes if we choose differently?"

knowledgeCheck.question: Superior Manufacturing is deciding whether to accept a special order for 1,000 units at a special price of $25 per unit. The normal selling price is $35 per unit. The company has excess capacity. Per unit costs are: Direct materials $8, Direct labor $5, Variable overhead $3, Fixed overhead $7, Selling expenses $2 (all variable). A supervisor will need to be on-site for the duration of the special order. The supervisor's salary is $60,000 per year. The special order will take 2 weeks. What is the total relevant cost for Superior to consider when deciding whether to accept the special order?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The relevant costs are the differential costs that will change if the special order is accepted. These include: Direct materials ($8 * 1,000 = $8,000), Direct labor ($5 * 1,000 = $5,000), Variable overhead ($3 * 1,000 = $3,000), Variable selling expenses ($2 * 1,000 = $2,000), and the additional supervisor cost. The supervisor's annual salary is $60,000, and the special order will take 2 weeks. Assuming 50 working weeks in a year, the supervisor cost is ($60,000 / 50) * 2 = $2,400. Therefore, the total relevant cost is $8,000 + $5,000 + $3,000 + $2,000 + $2,400 = $20,400. The fixed overhead is irrelevant as it is incurred regardless of accepting the special order. Note that the question requires you to calculate the *total* relevant cost to consider, not simply the per-unit relevant cost. Therefore, the per-unit costs must be multiplied by 1,000 to get the *total* relevant cost. $20,400 + ($8 * 1,000) + ($5 * 1,000) + ($3 * 1,000) + ($2 * 1,000) = $20,400
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to only include costs that will change as a result of accepting the special order. Consider if any currently fixed costs will change.

reveal.question: Define 'Opportunity Cost' in the context of managerial accounting.
reveal.answer: Opportunity cost is the potential benefit that is forfeited when one alternative is chosen over another. It represents the value of the next best alternative that is not selected.

rows.label: Definition
values: Costs that have already been incurred and cannot be recovered.
Future costs that differ between alternatives.

rows.label: Decision-Making
values: Irrelevant for future decisions.
Relevant for future decisions.

rows.label: Characteristics
values: Historical, unavoidable.
Future-oriented, differential.

title: Special Order Decisions
description: Analyze one-time orders at below-normal prices

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Should we accept a one-time order at a reduced price? This common scenario requires careful analysis of incremental costs and benefits. CMAs must separate relevant from irrelevant costs and consider capacity, opportunity costs, and strategic implications.

sections.title: Special Order Defined
sections.content:
**A special order is:**
• One-time or unusual order
• Often at a price below normal
• From a customer outside regular channels
• Won't affect regular sales

**Key question:**
Does the incremental revenue exceed the incremental cost?

**Decision rule:**
Accept if: Incremental Revenue > Incremental Cost
Reject if: Incremental Revenue < Incremental Cost

sections.title: Basic Special Order Analysis
sections.content:
**Example:**
Regular price: $100
Variable cost: $60
Fixed cost per unit (allocated): $25
Full cost: $85
Regular margin: $15

**Special order offer: $70 for 1,000 units**

Wrong thinking: "$70 < $85 cost, so reject."

**Correct analysis:**
Incremental revenue: $70 × 1,000 = $70,000
Incremental cost: $60 × 1,000 = $60,000
Incremental profit: **$10,000**

**ACCEPT!** (Fixed costs don't change)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Special Order Rule
sections.content:
**"Above VC = OK"**

If special order price > Variable cost per unit:
→ Accept (with excess capacity)

**Why?**
Fixed costs are paid either way
Any contribution above variable cost adds profit

**BUT: Consider capacity and opportunity cost!**

sections.title: Capacity Considerations
sections.content:
**With excess capacity:**
No opportunity cost → Compare to variable cost only

**At full capacity:**
Must give up regular sales → Include opportunity cost!

**Example at full capacity:**
Special order: 1,000 units at $70
Must sacrifice regular sales: 500 units at $100

Incremental revenue: $70 × 1,000 = $70,000
Incremental costs: $60 × 1,000 = $60,000
Lost contribution: ($100-$60) × 500 = **$20,000**
Net effect: $70,000 - $60,000 - $20,000 = **-$10,000**

**REJECT!**

sections.title: Qualitative Considerations
sections.content:
**Beyond the numbers, consider:**

**Risks of accepting:**
• Regular customers may demand lower prices
• May establish precedent for future orders
• Could damage brand/reputation
• Relationship with the customer

**Potential benefits:**
• Opens new market
• Builds relationship for future business
• Uses otherwise idle capacity
• Covers some fixed costs in slow periods

**These factors may override the quantitative analysis!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Which Costs Are Incremental?
sections.content:
**Include only costs that CHANGE:**

**Usually incremental:**
• Direct materials
• Direct labor (if paid per unit)
• Variable overhead
• Shipping (if company pays)
• Special tooling/setup

**Usually NOT incremental:**
• Fixed manufacturing overhead
• Fixed selling/admin costs
• Allocated corporate costs

**Read carefully for special circumstances!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Accept special order if Incremental Revenue > Incremental Cost
With excess capacity, compare to variable cost only
At full capacity, include opportunity cost of lost sales
Fixed costs usually NOT relevant (incurred anyway)
Consider incremental fixed costs if any (setup, tooling)
Qualitative factors may override quantitative analysis
Beware of setting precedent for lower prices

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is operating at 80% capacity. A special order for 500 units is received at a price of $50 per unit. The normal selling price is $75 per unit, variable costs are $40 per unit, and fixed costs are $20 per unit (based on normal capacity). Accepting the special order will:
options: Increase profit by $5,000
Decrease profit by $5,000
Increase profit by $15,000
Decrease profit by $15,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the company has excess capacity (operating at 80%), there is no opportunity cost. The incremental revenue is $50 * 500 = $25,000. The incremental cost is $40 * 500 = $20,000. Therefore, the increase in profit is $25,000 - $20,000 = $5,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the variable costs and the fact that the company has excess capacity.

reveal.question: List four characteristics of a special order.
reveal.answer: 1. One-time or unusual order. 2. Often at a price below normal. 3. From a customer outside regular channels. 4. Won't affect regular sales.

rows.label: Opportunity Cost
values: None
Present (Lost Regular Sales)

rows.label: Cost Comparison
values: Compare to variable cost
Compare to variable cost + opportunity cost

rows.label: Decision Rule
values: Accept if Incremental Revenue > Incremental Variable Cost
Accept if Incremental Revenue > Incremental Variable Cost + Opportunity Cost

title: Make or Buy Decisions
description: Analyze whether to manufacture internally or purchase from suppliers

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Make or buy decisions affect cost structure, quality control, and strategic flexibility. CMAs analyze the relevant costs of manufacturing vs. purchasing, considering both quantitative and qualitative factors. This is a fundamental outsourcing analysis.

sections.title: Make or Buy Framework
sections.content:
**Compare:**
Relevant cost to MAKE vs. Purchase cost to BUY

**Relevant costs to make:**
• Direct materials
• Direct labor
• Variable overhead
• Avoidable fixed costs
• Opportunity cost of capacity

**Costs to buy:**
• Purchase price
• Transportation/receiving
• Inspection
• Any unavoidable making costs

sections.title: Analyzing the Example
sections.content:
**Listed cost: Make $35 > Buy $28**
But wait - is all $10 of fixed overhead avoidable?

**Scenario 1:** Fixed overhead unavoidable
Relevant make cost: $8 + $12 + $5 = **$25**
Buy cost: $28
**Decision: MAKE** (saves $3/unit)

**Scenario 2:** $4 of fixed overhead avoidable
Relevant make cost: $25 + $4 = **$29**
Buy cost: $28
**Decision: BUY** (saves $1/unit)

**The answer depends on which costs are truly avoidable!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Make or Buy
sections.content:
**"AVO-CAD-O"** (like avocado!)

**AVO**idable costs to make
**C**ompare to
**A**ll costs to buy
**D**on't include unavoidable fixed costs!
**O**pportunity cost if capacity has alternative use

**Only AVOIDABLE costs are relevant!**

sections.title: Opportunity Cost of Freed Capacity
sections.content:
**If we buy, freed capacity could be used for:**
• Producing other profitable products
• Renting out the space
• Reducing fixed costs

**Example:**
Freed capacity can produce Product Z
Product Z contribution: $15,000/year

**Analysis (annual, 10,000 units):**
Make cost: $25 × 10,000 = $250,000
Buy cost: $28 × 10,000 = $280,000
Benefits of buying: Z contribution = $15,000

Net cost to make: $250,000 + $15,000 = $265,000
Net cost to buy: $280,000

**Still MAKE** (even considering opportunity cost)

sections.title: Qualitative Factors
sections.content:
**Arguments for making:**
• Quality control
• Reliable supply
• Protect proprietary technology
• Keep skilled workforce

**Arguments for buying:**
• Supplier expertise/economies of scale
• Flexibility in volume
• Focus on core competencies
• Reduce capital investment

**Strategic importance may override cost considerations!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Step-Fixed Costs
sections.content:
**If buying eliminates a step-fixed cost, it's relevant!**

**Example:**
Making requires a supervisor at $60,000/year
If we buy, we eliminate this position

**$60,000 is avoidable and RELEVANT!**

**Contrast:**
Factory manager's salary continues regardless
→ NOT relevant (unavoidable)

**Ask: "Does this cost GO AWAY if we buy?"**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Compare avoidable make costs to total buy costs
Allocated fixed costs are NOT relevant unless avoided
Include opportunity cost of freed capacity
Variable costs are typically relevant
Fixed costs are relevant only if truly eliminated
Qualitative factors may override cost analysis
Consider quality, reliability, strategic importance

sections.title: Make or Buy Decision: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is currently manufacturing a component with the following costs per unit: Direct Materials $5, Direct Labor $8, Variable Overhead $3, Fixed Overhead $6 ($2 avoidable). An outside supplier has offered to sell the component for $20 per unit. Assuming the company has no alternative use for the facilities currently used to manufacture the component, should the company make or buy the component?
options: Make the component; it is $2 cheaper per unit.
Buy the component; it is $2 cheaper per unit.
Make the component; it is $4 cheaper per unit.
Buy the component; it is $4 cheaper per unit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The relevant costs to make are: Direct Materials ($5) + Direct Labor ($8) + Variable Overhead ($3) + Avoidable Fixed Overhead ($2) = $18. The cost to buy is $20. Therefore, it is $2 cheaper to make the component.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to only consider relevant costs when making the make or buy decision.

reveal.question: List the categories of costs that are typically considered relevant when making a 'make' decision.
reveal.answer: The relevant costs to make include: Direct Materials, Direct Labor, Variable Overhead, Avoidable Fixed Costs, and Opportunity Cost of Capacity.

rows.label: Cost Focus
values: Relevant production costs (DM, DL, VOH, Avoidable FOH)
Purchase price + incremental receiving/inspection costs

rows.label: Capacity Utilization
values: Requires production capacity; opportunity cost if capacity can be used for something else
Frees up production capacity

rows.label: Control
values: Maintains control over production process and quality
Relies on supplier for quality and timely delivery

rows.label: Strategic Factors
values: May be crucial for maintaining core competencies
Potential for supply chain disruptions

title: Keep or Drop Decisions
description: Analyze whether to discontinue a product, segment, or service

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: A product looks unprofitable - should we drop it? CMAs must analyze whether eliminating a segment improves overall profit. The key is separating avoidable from unavoidable costs. Many "unprofitable" products actually contribute to covering fixed costs!

sections.title: Keep or Drop Decision Rule
sections.content:
**Drop if:**
Revenue lost < Costs avoided

**Keep if:**
Revenue lost > Costs avoided

**Or equivalently:**
Keep if segment's contribution margin > Avoidable fixed costs

**The key question:**
What costs actually DISAPPEAR if we drop this segment?

sections.title: Analyzing Product C
sections.content:
**Product C shows $(30,000) loss. Drop it?**

**If we drop C:**
Revenue lost: $200,000
Variable costs saved: $150,000
Direct fixed costs saved (assume avoidable): $60,000
Total costs saved: $210,000

**Effect on profit:**
Costs saved $210,000 > Revenue lost $200,000
**DROP Product C** - saves $10,000

**BUT WAIT - what if not all fixed costs are avoidable?**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Segment Margin
sections.content:
**"Segment Margin = Drop Decision"**

**Segment Margin = CM - Direct Fixed Costs**

**If segment margin is POSITIVE:**
→ Keep! It covers its own costs and contributes to common costs.

**If segment margin is NEGATIVE:**
→ Consider dropping (but verify fixed costs are truly avoidable!)

**Ignore allocated common costs for drop decision!**

sections.title: What if Fixed Costs Aren't Fully Avoidable?
sections.content:
**Reanalysis: Only $40,000 of C's $60,000 fixed costs are avoidable**

**If we drop C:**
Revenue lost: $200,000
Variable costs saved: $150,000
Avoidable fixed costs: $40,000
Total costs saved: $190,000

**Effect on profit:**
Costs saved $190,000 < Revenue lost $200,000
**KEEP Product C** - dropping costs $10,000!

**The remaining $20,000 will be reallocated to A and B!**

sections.title: Complementary Products Effect
sections.content:
**Sometimes products are complementary:**

• Dropping razors may reduce blade sales
• Dropping appetizers may reduce dinner sales
• Dropping entry-level product may lose future upgraders

**Include lost contribution from related products!**

**Example:**
Drop C saves $10,000 (per earlier analysis)
But C customers also buy $100,000 of A at 40% CM
Lost A contribution: $40,000

Net effect: ($10,000) - ($40,000) = **$(30,000) loss**

**KEEP Product C!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Common Cost Allocation
sections.content:
**IGNORE allocated common costs for drop decisions!**

**Why?**
Common costs continue regardless of which segments exist.
They just get reallocated to remaining segments.

**Example:**
Total common costs: $100,000
Allocated to C: $20,000

If C is dropped, the $100,000 doesn't change!
It's just split between A and B now.

**Common costs are NOT relevant to the drop decision!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Drop if: Revenue lost < Costs avoided
Segment Margin = CM - Direct (Traceable) Fixed Costs
Positive segment margin = Keep the segment
Only AVOIDABLE costs are relevant
Ignore allocated common costs (continue either way)
Consider complementary product effects
Verify which fixed costs truly disappear

sections.title: Keep or Drop Decision: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A has a contribution margin of $100,000 and avoidable fixed costs of $80,000. Unavoidable fixed costs are $40,000. If Division A is dropped, what will be the impact on the company's overall profit?
options: Decrease of $100,000
Decrease of $20,000
Increase of $20,000
No change
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The company will lose the contribution margin of $100,000 but save $80,000 in avoidable fixed costs. The net effect is a decrease in profit of $20,000 ($100,000 - $80,000). Unavoidable fixed costs are irrelevant to the decision.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus only on avoidable costs and the contribution margin.

reveal.question: State the 'Keep or Drop' decision rule based on revenue lost and costs avoided.
reveal.answer: Drop if Revenue lost < Costs avoided; Keep if Revenue lost > Costs avoided

rows.label: Definition
values: Costs that will disappear if a segment is dropped.
Costs that will continue to be incurred even if a segment is dropped.

rows.label: Relevance to Keep/Drop Decision
values: Relevant; used in the analysis.
Irrelevant; not used in the analysis.

rows.label: Example
values: Direct fixed costs like a segment manager's salary if they're laid off.
Allocated overhead costs like corporate headquarters rent.

title: Sell or Process Further
description: Determine optimal point to sell joint products

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Joint products emerge from a common process. Should we sell at split-off or process further? CMAs analyze the incremental revenue vs. incremental cost of additional processing. Joint costs are sunk for this decision!

sections.title: Joint Costs and Split-Off Point
sections.content:
**Joint costs:** Costs incurred before products become separately identifiable

**Split-off point:** Where joint products become separate and identifiable

**Example:**
Oil refinery: Crude oil → Gasoline, Diesel, Kerosene
(Split-off is where products separate)

**Key insight:**
Joint costs are SUNK for sell/process further decisions!
They're incurred regardless of what happens after split-off.

sections.title: Decision Rule
sections.content:
**Process further if:**
Incremental Revenue > Incremental (Separable) Costs

**Compare:**
• Revenue at split-off
• Revenue if processed further
• Costs of additional processing

**DO NOT include joint costs!**
They're the same regardless of the decision.

**Focus only on what happens AFTER split-off!**

sections.title: Analyzing Each Product
sections.content:
**Product X:**
Incremental revenue: $80,000 - $50,000 = $30,000
Incremental cost: $20,000
Incremental profit: **$10,000** → PROCESS FURTHER

**Product Y:**
Incremental revenue: $42,000 - $30,000 = $12,000
Incremental cost: $15,000
Incremental loss: **$(3,000)** → SELL AT SPLIT-OFF

**Product Z:**
Incremental revenue: $35,000 - $20,000 = $15,000
Incremental cost: $18,000
Incremental loss: **$(3,000)** → SELL AT SPLIT-OFF

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sell or Process
sections.content:
**"JCS" - Joint Costs are SUNK!**

**J**oint costs = Already incurred
**C**ompare only what's AFTER split-off
**S**eparable costs vs. Incremental revenue

**If incremental revenue > separable cost → Process further**
**If incremental revenue < separable cost → Sell at split-off**

**Never include joint costs in this decision!**

sections.title: By-Products
sections.content:
**By-product:** Minor product with relatively small sales value

**Accounting treatments:**

**Net realizable value method:**
NRV of by-product reduces joint cost of main products

**Recognition at sale:**
Revenue recognized when by-product is sold

**For sell/process decisions:**
Analyze by-products the same way:
Process further only if incremental revenue > incremental cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Don't Allocate Joint Costs for Decisions
sections.content:
**Joint cost allocation is for:**
• Inventory valuation
• External financial reporting

**Joint cost allocation is NOT for:**
• Sell/process decisions
• Product profitability analysis

**Why?**
Allocation methods (sales value, physical units, NRV) are arbitrary.
They don't reflect true product costs.

**For decisions: Focus ONLY on separable costs!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Joint costs are SUNK - ignore for sell/process decisions
Compare: Revenue increase vs. Additional processing cost
Process further if incremental revenue > separable cost
Each product is analyzed independently
Different products may have different optimal decisions
By-products analyzed the same way as main products
Don't use allocated joint costs for decision-making

sections.title: Joint Costs and the Sell or Process Further Decision

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the decision to sell a joint product at the split-off point or process it further?
options: Joint costs should be allocated to the joint products to determine profitability before making the decision.
The decision should be based solely on whether the additional revenue from further processing exceeds the total cost of production, including joint costs.
The decision to process further should be based on whether the incremental revenue from further processing exceeds the incremental processing costs.
Joint costs are relevant in the decision to sell or process further because they impact the overall profitability of the company.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that the decision to process further should be based on whether the incremental revenue from further processing exceeds the incremental processing costs. Joint costs are sunk costs and irrelevant to the sell or process further decision.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the key insight about sunk costs!

sections.title: Recall: The Golden Rule of 'Sell or Process Further'

reveal.question: State the decision rule for determining whether to process a joint product further after the split-off point.
reveal.answer: Process further if the incremental revenue from further processing is greater than the incremental (separable) costs of further processing.

rows.label: Cost Consideration
values: Joint costs are incurred.
Incremental (separable) costs are incurred. Joint costs are irrelevant.

rows.label: Revenue Impact
values: Revenue is generated at the initial sales price.
Potential for increased revenue due to product enhancement.

rows.label: Decision Focus
values: Initial decision point for product separation.
Decision to increase product value through additional processing.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company produces two joint products, A and B. At the split-off point, Product A can be sold for $20,000 and Product B can be sold for $30,000. If Product A is processed further, it can be sold for $35,000 at an additional cost of $10,000. If Product B is processed further, it can be sold for $40,000 at an additional cost of $12,000. Which product(s) should be processed further?
options: Only Product A.
Only Product B.
Both Product A and Product B.
Neither Product A nor Product B.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Product A: Incremental Revenue = $35,000 - $20,000 = $15,000; Incremental Cost = $10,000. Process further since $15,000 > $10,000. Product B: Incremental Revenue = $40,000 - $30,000 = $10,000; Incremental Cost = $12,000. Do not process further since $10,000 < $12,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the incremental revenue and incremental cost for each product separately.

title: Pricing Strategies
description: Understand pricing methods and strategic pricing decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Pricing is one of the most powerful profit levers. CMAs provide cost data for pricing decisions, analyze pricing strategies, and understand how different approaches affect profitability. The right price balances cost recovery, competitive positioning, and customer value.

sections.title: Pricing Approaches
sections.content:
**Cost-based pricing:**
Start with costs, add markup for profit

**Market-based pricing:**
Start with what market will pay, adjust costs accordingly

**Value-based pricing:**
Price based on value delivered to customer

**Competition-based pricing:**
Set prices relative to competitors

**Each has its place depending on market conditions!**

sections.title: Cost-Plus Pricing
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Price = Cost + (Markup % × Cost)

**Which cost base?**
• Variable cost (contribution approach)
• Absorption cost (full cost)
• Total cost including SG&A

**Example (absorption cost):**
Full manufacturing cost: $80
Desired markup: 25%
Price: $80 × 1.25 = **$100**

**Advantages:** Simple, ensures cost recovery
**Disadvantages:** Ignores market, demand, competition

sections.title: Markup Calculation
sections.content:
**Finding required markup:**

Markup % = Desired Profit / Cost Base

**Example:**
Target ROI: 15%
Invested capital: $1,000,000
Expected units: 10,000
Cost per unit: $75

Desired profit: 15% × $1,000,000 = $150,000
Profit per unit: $150,000 / 10,000 = $15
Markup %: $15 / $75 = **20%**
Price: $75 × 1.20 = **$90**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Pricing Methods
sections.content:
**"CMVT"** - Four Pricing Philosophies:

**C**ost-plus: Add margin to cost (inside-out)
**M**arket: What will market pay? (outside-in)
**V**alue: What is it worth to customer?
**T**arget: Market price - profit = Allowable cost

**Cost-plus is easiest but ignores what customers will pay!
Market and value approaches focus on the customer.**

sections.title: Target Costing
sections.content:
**Market-driven approach:**

**Steps:**
1. Determine market price customers will pay
2. Subtract required profit margin
3. Result = Maximum allowable cost
4. Design/engineer to meet cost target

**Formula:**
Target Cost = Market Price - Required Profit

**Example:**
Market price: $200
Required margin: 20%
Required profit: $40
Target cost: $200 - $40 = **$160**

*If we can't make it for $160, don't make it!*

sections.title: Price Elasticity of Demand
sections.content:
**Elasticity = % Change in Quantity / % Change in Price**

**Elastic demand (E > 1):**
• Price cut increases revenue
• Price hike decreases revenue
• Customers are price-sensitive

**Inelastic demand (E < 1):**
• Price cut decreases revenue
• Price hike increases revenue
• Customers less price-sensitive

**Example:** E = 2
10% price cut → 20% more quantity → Revenue increases!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Long-Run vs. Short-Run Pricing
sections.content:
**Short-run minimum price:**
Variable cost per unit (contribution pricing)
→ Any price above VC contributes to fixed costs

**Long-run minimum price:**
Full cost (covers all costs including fixed)
→ Must recover ALL costs to be sustainable

**Special orders may accept short-run pricing
Regular business needs long-run pricing!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cost-plus: Price = Cost × (1 + Markup%)
Target costing: Target Cost = Market Price - Required Profit
Market-based pricing starts with customer willingness to pay
Price elasticity determines revenue impact of price changes
Short-run floor = Variable cost; Long-run floor = Full cost
Price skimming (high initial) vs. Penetration (low initial)
Different situations call for different pricing strategies

knowledgeCheck.question: A company uses absorption cost-plus pricing. Its full manufacturing cost per unit is $50, and its selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses are $10 per unit. The company desires a 20% markup on total cost. What is the selling price per unit?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the total cost per unit: $50 (manufacturing cost) + $10 (SG&A) = $60.  Then, apply the 20% markup: $60 * 1.20 = $72.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include all relevant costs in the cost base before applying the markup.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the required markup percentage in cost-plus pricing?
reveal.answer: Markup % = Desired Profit / Cost Base. This formula determines the percentage markup needed to achieve a target profit level based on a chosen cost base.

rows.label: Starting Point
values: Internal Costs
Market Demand/Competitor Prices

rows.label: Focus
values: Covering Costs and Achieving Desired Profit
Meeting Customer Needs and Competitive Pressures

rows.label: Price Determination
values: Cost + Markup
Market Price - Desired Profit Margin or Cost Adjustments

rows.label: Suitability
values: Products with less competition, cost control is essential
Highly competitive markets, customer value is key

title: Transfer Pricing
description: Establish prices for internal transfers between divisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Divisions within a company often buy from each other. At what price? Transfer pricing affects divisional profits, taxes, and decision-making incentives. CMAs must set transfer prices that motivate goal-congruent behavior while fairly evaluating performance.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Objectives
sections.content:
**Good transfer prices should:**

• **Goal congruence:** Align divisional and corporate interests
• **Performance evaluation:** Fairly measure divisional profit
• **Autonomy:** Preserve divisional decision-making
• **Tax optimization:** Minimize overall tax burden (legally!)

**The challenge:**
No single method achieves all objectives perfectly!

sections.title: General Transfer Pricing Rule
sections.content:
**Minimum transfer price (seller's floor):**
Variable cost + Opportunity cost (lost CM on external sales)

**Maximum transfer price (buyer's ceiling):**
Market price (or cost of buying externally)

**Transfer price range:**
Minimum ≤ TP ≤ Maximum

**Key insight:**
Transfer should occur if seller's minimum < buyer's maximum
(Value is created by internal transfer)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Transfer Price Range
sections.content:
**"VOC to MARket"**

**Floor (seller's minimum):**
**V**ariable cost + **O**pportunity **C**ost

**Ceiling (buyer's maximum):**
**MAR**ket price (what buyer would pay externally)

**If floor > ceiling:**
No transfer should occur!

**If floor < ceiling:**
Transfer at any price in the range is OK for the company.

sections.title: Transfer Pricing Example
sections.content:
**Division A (seller) data:**
Variable cost: $30
Full cost: $45
Market price: $50
Capacity: 10,000 units
External demand: 8,000 units

**With excess capacity (2,000 units):**
Seller's minimum: $30 + $0 = **$30** (no opportunity cost)

**At full capacity:**
Seller's minimum: $30 + ($50-$30) = **$50** (lost CM)

**Division B should buy internally only if their max > seller's min!**

sections.title: Goal Congruence Issues
sections.content:
**Problem with variable cost transfer price:**
Seller shows no profit → No motivation to sell internally

**Problem with cost-plus:**
Passes inefficiencies to buyer
No incentive for seller to control costs

**Problem with market price:**
May not exist for intermediate products
Ignores synergies of internal transfer

**Solution often requires negotiation or dual pricing!**

sections.title: International Transfer Pricing
sections.content:
**Tax authorities are watching!**

Companies may set transfer prices to:
• Shift profit to low-tax jurisdictions
• Minimize overall tax burden

**Arms-length principle:**
Transfer prices should be what unrelated parties would pay.

**Methods accepted by IRS/OECD:**
• Comparable uncontrolled price (CUP)
• Resale price method
• Cost-plus method
• Transactional net margin method (TNMM)

**Documentation is critical!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Capacity Matters!
sections.content:
**The selling division's situation changes the analysis:**

**Excess capacity:**
• Opportunity cost = $0
• Minimum TP = Variable cost
• Internal transfer almost always makes sense

**At full capacity:**
• Opportunity cost = Lost CM from external sales
• Minimum TP = VC + Lost CM
• May equal market price!

**Always check capacity before calculating!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Minimum TP = Variable cost + Opportunity cost
Maximum TP = External market price
Transfer should occur if minimum < maximum
Excess capacity → Opportunity cost = $0
Full capacity → Opportunity cost = Lost contribution margin
No single method achieves all objectives
International transfers must follow arm's-length principle

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary objective of establishing a transfer pricing policy within a decentralized organization?
options: To minimize the overall tax burden of the corporation.
To maximize the autonomy of divisional managers.
To ensure that all intercompany transfers are recorded at cost.
To fairly evaluate the performance of individual divisions.
E. To align divisional and corporate interests.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While recording transfers might involve costs, the objective isn't simply to record them 'at cost.' Effective transfer pricing aims for goal congruence, performance evaluation, autonomy, and tax optimization. Simply recording at cost could distort divisional profitability and hinder informed decision-making.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the broad goals that transfer pricing aims to achieve for the entire organization, not just accounting procedures.

reveal.question: What components make up the minimum transfer price (seller's floor)?
reveal.answer: The minimum transfer price is calculated as the sum of the seller's Variable Cost and Opportunity Cost (lost Contribution Margin on external sales).

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A can sell a component externally for $75. Its variable cost to produce the component is $40. Division B needs this component and can either buy it externally for $80 or from Division A. What is the transfer price range?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The minimum transfer price (seller's floor) is the variable cost ($40) plus the opportunity cost. Since Division A can sell externally for $75, that's their lost revenue, so the minimum price is $40. The maximum transfer price (buyer's ceiling) is the market price if bought externally, which is $80. Therefore, the transfer price range is $40-$80.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the best alternative for both the buying and selling divisions.

title: Constrained Resource Decisions
description: Optimize product mix when resources are limited

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Every company faces constraints - machine hours, labor, materials, or space. CMAs must determine the optimal product mix when resources are limited. The key: maximize contribution margin per unit of the scarce resource, not per unit of product!

sections.title: The Resource Constraint Problem
sections.content:
**When you can't make all the products you can sell:**

• What limits you? (the constraint/bottleneck)
• How should you allocate that resource?
• Which products should get priority?

**Key insight:**
Maximize CM per unit of the SCARCE RESOURCE,
not CM per unit of product!

**A product with lower CM per unit may be better
if it uses less of the scarce resource!**

sections.title: Analysis of Example
sections.content:
**Available machine hours: 8,000
Demand: Unlimited for both products**

**Wrong approach (CM per unit):**
Make A ($40 CM) before B ($30 CM)

**Right approach (CM per constraint):**
B: $30 ÷ 2 hours = **$15/hour**
A: $40 ÷ 4 hours = **$10/hour**

**Make B first!**
With 8,000 hours: 4,000 units of B × $30 = $120,000
OR: 2,000 units of A × $40 = $80,000

**$40,000 more by prioritizing B!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Constraint Optimization
sections.content:
**"CM per CB"**

**C**ontribution **M**argin per **C**onstraint unit = **B**est product!

NOT CM per unit!
NOT highest selling price!
NOT lowest cost!

**The scarce resource is your bottleneck - use it wisely!**

Rank products by CM / Resource requirement
Make highest-ranked products first.

sections.title: Multiple Constraints
sections.content:
**When multiple resources are scarce:**

**Simple approach:**
Identify the BINDING constraint (most limiting)
Optimize for that constraint

**Complex approach:**
Linear programming (LP) required

**LP model:**
Maximize: Total CM = CM₁X₁ + CM₂X₂ + ...
Subject to:
Constraint 1: a₁X₁ + a₂X₂ ≤ Available₁
Constraint 2: b₁X₁ + b₂X₂ ≤ Available₂
X₁, X₂ ≥ 0

sections.title: Theory of Constraints (TOC)
sections.content:
**Goldratt's approach:**

**Five focusing steps:**
1. **Identify** the constraint
2. **Exploit** the constraint (maximize its output)
3. **Subordinate** everything else to the constraint
4. **Elevate** the constraint (add capacity)
5. **Repeat** (find the new constraint)

**Key metric:**
Throughput = Sales - Truly Variable Costs

**Maximize throughput per constraint unit!**

sections.title: Practical Application
sections.content:
**Steps for constrained resource problem:**

1. Identify the constraint (what's limiting output?)
2. Calculate CM per unit of constraint for each product
3. Rank products by CM per constraint (highest first)
4. Allocate constraint to highest-ranked product up to demand
5. Continue to next product until constraint exhausted
6. Calculate total contribution margin

**Watch for demand limits - you can't sell more than market wants!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Demand Constraints
sections.content:
**Don't make more than you can sell!**

**Example:**
Product B: CM/hour = $15, Demand = 2,000 units
Product A: CM/hour = $10, Demand = 3,000 units
Available hours: 8,000

**Correct approach:**
Make B first: 2,000 × 2 hours = 4,000 hours used
Remaining hours: 4,000 for A
Make A: 4,000 ÷ 4 = 1,000 units

Total CM: (2,000 × $30) + (1,000 × $40) = **$100,000**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Maximize CM per unit of SCARCE RESOURCE
Rank products by CM/constraint, not CM/unit
Allocate scarce resource to highest-ranked products first
Respect demand constraints - can't sell infinite units
Multiple constraints may require linear programming
TOC: Identify, Exploit, Subordinate, Elevate, Repeat
The constraint determines maximum throughput

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X manufactures two products, Alpha and Beta. Alpha has a contribution margin of $50 per unit and requires 5 machine hours. Beta has a contribution margin of $40 per unit and requires 2 machine hours.  Company X has a limited capacity of 1,000 machine hours.  Assuming demand for both products is unlimited, how many units of Beta should Company X produce to maximize its profit?
options: 0 units
200 units
400 units
500 units
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To maximize profit, we need to focus on the contribution margin per machine hour. Alpha: $50 / 5 hours = $10/hour. Beta: $40 / 2 hours = $20/hour.  Beta is more profitable per machine hour. Therefore, allocate all 1,000 hours to Beta: 1,000 hours / 2 hours per unit = 500 units.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the contribution margin per machine hour for each product.

reveal.question: What is the primary decision rule when allocating a constrained resource?
reveal.answer: Maximize contribution margin per unit of the SCARCE RESOURCE, not contribution margin per unit of product.

sections.title: CM per Unit vs. CM per Constrained Resource

rows.label: Focus
values: Profitability of each individual product
Profitability of each unit of the limited resource

rows.label: Application
values: Useful when resources are NOT constrained
Crucial when resources are limited and demand exceeds capacity

rows.label: Calculation
values: Selling Price - Variable Costs
(Selling Price - Variable Costs) / Units of Constrained Resource

title: Decision Analysis Under Uncertainty
description: Make decisions when outcomes are uncertain using probability analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Business decisions are rarely certain. CMAs must incorporate uncertainty into analysis using expected value, decision trees, and sensitivity analysis. Understanding how to analyze risky decisions leads to better choices under ambiguity.

sections.title: Expected Value
sections.content:
**EV = Σ (Outcome × Probability)**

**Example:**
New product investment:
• 30% probability: $500,000 profit
• 50% probability: $200,000 profit
• 20% probability: $(100,000) loss

EV = (0.30 × $500,000) + (0.50 × $200,000) + (0.20 × -$100,000)
EV = $150,000 + $100,000 - $20,000
EV = **$230,000**

**Decision:** If EV > 0 or EV > alternative, accept.

sections.title: Decision Tree Structure
sections.content:
**Components:**

**Decision nodes (squares):** Points where you choose
**Chance nodes (circles):** Points where uncertainty resolves
**Branches:** Represent alternatives or outcomes
**Payoffs:** End values at branch tips

**Analysis (work backwards):**
1. Calculate EV at each chance node
2. Choose best alternative at decision nodes
3. Roll back to starting point

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Decision Trees
sections.content:
**"Squares for STRATEGY, Circles for CHANCE"**

□ = You decide (pick one branch)
○ = Nature decides (weight by probability)

**Analyze from RIGHT to LEFT:**
1. Start at outcomes (payoffs)
2. Weight by probability at circles
3. Choose best option at squares
4. Work back to root

**The path through best choices = Optimal strategy**

sections.title: Decision Tree Example
sections.content:
**Decision:** Launch product A or B?

**Product A:**
• 60%: Success → $300,000
• 40%: Failure → $(50,000)
EV(A) = (0.60 × 300,000) + (0.40 × -50,000) = **$160,000**

**Product B:**
• 80%: Success → $200,000
• 20%: Failure → $0
EV(B) = (0.80 × 200,000) + (0.20 × 0) = **$160,000**

**Same EV! But B has less variance (less risky).**

sections.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sections.content:
**What if assumptions change?**

**Steps:**
1. Identify key variables
2. Change one variable at a time
3. Recalculate outcome
4. Determine "trigger point" where decision changes

**Example:**
If success probability for A drops below 53.3%,
EV(A) falls below EV(B).

**Sensitivity analysis answers:**
"How wrong can our estimates be before we'd decide differently?"

sections.title: Value of Information
sections.content:
**Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI):**
EVPI = EV with perfect info - EV without info

**What would you pay for a crystal ball?**

**Example:**
Without info: EV = $160,000 (best we can do)
With perfect info:
• If knew success (60%): Choose A for $300,000
• If knew failure (40%): Choose B for $0
EV with info = (0.60 × $300,000) + (0.40 × $0) = $180,000

EVPI = $180,000 - $160,000 = **$20,000**

**Would pay up to $20,000 for perfect prediction!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Risk Preferences
sections.content:
**Expected value assumes risk neutrality!**

**Risk-averse:** Would choose certain outcome over risky one with same EV
(Most individuals, especially with large amounts)

**Risk-neutral:** Indifferent between certain and risky with same EV
(Companies often assumed risk-neutral)

**Risk-seeking:** Prefer risky option with same EV
(Gamblers, some entrepreneurs)

**Real decisions may incorporate risk preferences beyond EV!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Expected Value = Σ(Outcome × Probability)
Decision trees: Squares for decisions, Circles for chance
Analyze decision trees from right to left (backward)
Choose option with highest expected value
Sensitivity analysis tests impact of changing assumptions
EVPI = EV with perfect info - EV without info
Risk preferences may override pure EV analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is considering launching a new product. Market research suggests a 40% chance of a $800,000 profit, a 35% chance of a $300,000 profit, and a 25% chance of a $200,000 loss. What is the expected value of launching this product?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to multiply each outcome by its probability and sum the results. Account for losses as negative values.

reveal.question: What are the four key components of a decision tree?
reveal.answer: The four key components of a decision tree are: 1. Decision nodes (squares), 2. Chance nodes (circles), 3. Branches, and 4. Payoffs.

knowledgeCheck.question: When analyzing a decision tree, in what direction do you typically work?
options: Forward, from the initial decision to the payoffs.
Backward, from the payoffs to the initial decision.
Randomly, as the order doesn't matter.
From the chance nodes to the decision nodes.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Decision tree analysis involves working backward from the payoffs to the initial decision. This allows you to calculate the expected value at each chance node and make informed decisions at each decision node.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how you would solve a puzzle – often starting with the end result and working your way back.
